reasoning: The `encrypt` function is part of the public API, exported directly from xmlenc.js and re-exported via the package main entry (index.js). The vulnerable parameter `options.pem` is a *required* option with no default — callers must supply it, but there is zero validation beyond a regex extraction that admits all XML-significant characters. The injection point is `keyinfo.tpl.xml.js` line 10, where `${encryptionPublicCert}` is interpolated without `escapehtml()` (all other interpolations in the same file use it). No schema validator, allowlist, or sanitizer guards `options.pem` before it reaches the XML sink. No non-default flag is required to trigger the vulnerability — it fires on every normal call to `encrypt()` with a crafted PEM. In real applications (SAML SPs, multi-tenant encryption services) that derive the PEM from an external or user-influenced source, an attacker can inject arbitrary XML into the output document.
